points.text: Reproduce checkout flow locally and in staging; capture request/response timestamps and DB logs.
Enable detailed query logging; measure execution time of the new join and compare to baseline.
Run EXPLAIN on the problematic query; add missing indexes or rewrite join to reduce row scans.
Verify payment API calls are wrapped in a transaction and include a unique idempotency key.
Add server‑side deduplication: reject duplicate payment requests within a short time window.
Replace client‑side blind retries with exponential backoff and limit retries to two attempts.
Implement a circuit breaker around the payment service to prevent overload during latency spikes.
Deploy monitoring: latency histogram, error rate, and double‑charge alerts; roll back the join if needed.
Enable distributed tracing on checkout flow to measure each service’s latency.
Profile the new SQL join with EXPLAIN to see if it causes full table scans or missing indexes.
Add DB indexes on columns used in the join predicates to reduce query time.
Instrument request timeout budgets; ensure client timeout < server processing time to avoid premature retries.
Implement idempotency keys for payment requests to prevent double charges on retries.
Introduce a circuit breaker around the payment service to stop retries when latency spikes.
Run load test replicating retry patterns to observe latency and error rates under stress.
Monitor DB connection pool usage; increase pool size or reduce long-running queries if saturated.
Enable pg_stat_statements and identify the checkout query’s total runtime and call count.
Capture the exact SQL with the new join; run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) to see plan cost and row estimates.
Verify that all join columns have appropriate B‑tree indexes; add missing indexes on foreign keys.
Check for sequential scans on large tables; if present, adjust statistics or create covering indexes.
Look for high lock wait times in pg_locks; consider SELECT … FOR UPDATE or reducing transaction scope.
Ensure the checkout transaction is idempotent: use a unique payment token or constraint to prevent double charges.
Add a retry‑safe pattern: store a payment attempt ID and reject duplicates at the DB level.
Review client retry back‑off settings; avoid immediate retries that cause contention.
Compare query plan before and after the join change to confirm no unexpected nested loop over large tables.
If needed, rewrite the join (e.g., use EXISTS or lateral join) to improve selectivity.
Capture slow query logs; examine the new join’s execution plan and add missing indexes.
Enable DB lock‑wait statistics; look for high lock wait times or deadlocks on order/payment tables.
Add a unique constraint (or index) on (order_id, payment_attempt) to make inserts idempotent.
Wrap payment record creation in a transaction with SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on the order row.
Use READ COMMITTED (or REPEATABLE READ) isolation to avoid dirty reads that cause double charges.
Implement client‑side idempotency tokens so retries do not create duplicate payment rows.
Temporarily disable the new join via feature flag, benchmark checkout latency, then redeploy with fixes.
Enable load balancer request tracing to capture latency and HTTP status codes for the checkout endpoint.
Inspect LB timeout settings and queue depth; ensure they exceed the expected DB query time after the new join.
Cross‑reference LB 502/504 logs with DB slow‑query logs to identify backend timeouts.
Check client retry policy and add an idempotency token to prevent double‑charge on retries.
Profile the new SQL join with EXPLAIN and measure execution time under realistic load.
Temporarily lower client retry count and monitor duplicate‑charge frequency.
Monitor Linux TCP retransmits and SYN backlog metrics for signs of network or socket overload.
Instrument checkout to capture latency percentiles (p95, p99) and request rate via tracing or metrics.
Apply Little’s Law (L = λ·W) using observed throughput (λ) and avg latency (W) to estimate concurrent load.
Budget total timeout: client retry timeout = observed p99 latency + network RTT + safety margin (e.g., 20%).
Profile the new SQL join with EXPLAIN; check row counts, add missing indexes, and estimate added query cost.
Limit client retries or make them idempotent; use back‑off and max‑retry count to avoid duplicate charges.
Introduce an idempotency key or payment token at the charge step to safely deduplicate repeated attempts.
Enable request/response logging with correlation IDs to trace each checkout attempt and retry path.
Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the new join query; add missing indexes to bring execution time under 200 ms.
Confirm DB transaction isolation (e.g., READ COMMITTED) and use SELECT … FOR UPDATE on payment rows.
Add a unique constraint or idempotency token column on orders/payments to block duplicate inserts.
Require clients to send an idempotency key; reject repeat checkouts with the same key within a short window.
Adjust client retry logic to back‑off only on network failures, not on HTTP 4xx responses.
Set up alerts for >2 duplicate‑charge events per minute to catch regressions quickly.
